author: @sadaavedan
text: Before the advent of the 3D printer, rapid protyping used two different techniques. One striking an appropriate polymer solution with a laser which makes the area hit by the laser to become solid.  Then the tray contains the polymer solution is lowered by a fraction and the process repeated. A second method was depositing a powdered material layer by layer and heating it up to melt into a solid. These machines were expensive. What you described seems to be related to these. Today the 3D printing made most of these obsolete.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the first one you mention is stereolithographic printing mate and the second is selective laser sintering - they are still very current though expensive - there was an attempt to bring stereolithographic printing the home market but I am unsure where that is right now. Filament type printing is popular because it is cheap - the slice and glue layer technique was tried with plastic rolls and Mcor have a machine that uses 80gsm office paper. It is a pretty active field - I don't think they are obsolete - but I may be wrong - cheers

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I haven't heard of this, either!!  Is there anything special about laser cutter rigs, or would this work equally as well with a laser cutting attachment on a CNC router?  I've been considering a DIY CNC for some time - this definitely gives me incentive to give it more serious thought.  Love 3D printing, but there's some things you just can't do very well.  (Transparency, for example.)  Thanks very much for sharing this with us, Rob!!   It's a spectacular find!!  🥰🥰🥰

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Good one.  Weirdly, just today I saw an news item about layering graphene on the surface of of kaptan tape using a laser.  Now I'm thinking that the laser might make it possible to convert the surface of almost any carbohydrate material to conductive graphene.  With your video, it seems as if there may be a way to create and "print" electronic circuits and devices of almost any shape using the laser.
